messages.author: Robin - M0JQQ - IO95ec
messages.text: A vertical antenna is essentially omnidirectional. imagine a slightly flattened ball with the antenna as the axis. As you increase the antenna gain, the ball gets squashed top and bottom into a donut shape at first, progressing to a fat disk at the higher gains. At low gain your Tx goes out in all directions (Rx comes in from all directions too). You will also get out to receivers which are both higher and lower than your antenna. As you increase antenna gain the donut shape reduces sensitivity of those higher and lower stations. So it is always a compromise.
messages.content_html: A vertical antenna is essentially omnidirectional. imagine a slightly flattened ball with the antenna as the axis. As you increase the antenna gain, the ball gets squashed top and bottom into a donut shape at first, progressing to a fat disk at the higher gains. At low gain your Tx goes out in all directions (Rx comes in from all directions too). You will also get out to receivers which are both higher and lower than your antenna. As you increase antenna gain the donut shape reduces sensitivity of those higher and lower stations. So it is always a compromise.
messages.reactions_html: 1

The Viterbi algorithm is a dynamic programming algorithm that finds the most likely sequence of hidden events that would explain a sequence of observed events. messages.author: EvilBlueSmartie
messages.text: I'd be looking at pulling stuff out of App.tsx into distinct components, hooks etc. You have a lot of state there too that could either be scoped to the components that use it (if not shared) or wrapped in a context provider. Things like the SVGs and that can be very easily moved into separate components, for example. For an application of this size, App.tsx should just be an entry point really, not where all your business logic and state lives.

messages.author: Bye Andy
messages.forwarded_html:
Forwarded
I know what your probably thinking, why do we need another packet analyzer. Well I started it as a local project for Teesside in the UK, but as we started receiving packets from the wider mesh again it started to really expand so I made the project UK wide, you can check it out on https://www.ukmesh.com, the live map is what has had the most work done to it. This is purely data that has arrived to my node in Teesside , so the data is very heavily skewed so shortly im going to be opening it up to some select areas around the UK to see how the site copes. The part of my project that I think really improves on prior maps is the links it can draw between nodes, it seems quite accurate so far! One of the things on my list is to improve the coverage map filter massively, I am aware how inaccurate the method is that I am using currently. its also open source on github at https://github.com/gadgethd/teesside-mesh im unsure on how well it will build on different machines but that is something we can get to when the time comes. *Also yes this project is quite similar to meshrank.net I am aware, I only found that site after starting mine up Anyway its very late here in the UK when im posting this so ill be going to bed
Originally sent: 2026-03-04 8:59 p.m.

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: You don't need to worry about a heat sink on the board, they can cope with >85C. If you have a battery you want to keep that below 45C which may be pushing it in a loft on the worst days. Ideally if it is plugged in and you just want a more stable supply you would use a Lithium-ion capacitor. With USB power v3 needed this v4 I think is ok without.
messages.content_html: You don't need to worry about a heat sink on the board, they can cope with >85C. If you have a battery you want to keep that below 45C which may be pushing it in a loft on the worst days. Ideally if it is plugged in and you just want a more stable supply you would use a Lithium-ion capacitor. With USB power v3 needed this v4 I think is ok without.

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: set tx 30 does nothing, the maximum the radio chip will accept is 22. If you enter set tx 1 then set tx 30 followed by get tx it will say 30 but be set to 1.
messages.content_html: set tx 30 does nothing, the maximum the radio chip will accept is 22. If you enter set tx 1 then set tx 30 followed by get tx it will say 30 but be set to 1.
messages.reply_html:
Caoboth
Well thats the NE38 repeater switched from a heltec v3 to a v4 and the tx set to 30 so hopefully a bit more reach

messages.author: Caoboth
messages.text: 22 is the limit on a heltec v3 30 is on v4
messages.content_html: 22 is the limit on a heltec v3 30 is on v4

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: Not it the software it isnt. 22 is the limit of the SX1262 chip, the radiolib code for some reason accepts more and doesn't tell you but the chip ignores it. On a v4 and some other boards there is an extra PA upping it to 28+-1. It is very confusing and doesn't need to be but the argumant was that the software cannot possibly know how all the boards work so it is up the the user to do the conversion. (edited)
messages.content_html: Not it the software it isnt. 22 is the limit of the SX1262 chip, the radiolib code for some reason accepts more and doesn't tell you but the chip ignores it. On a v4 and some other boards there is an extra PA upping it to 28+-1. It is very confusing and doesn't need to be but the argumant was that the software cannot possibly know how all the boards work so it is up the the user to do the conversion. (edited)

messages.author: Caoboth

messages.content_html: tbh fella it seems to be working well i got to admit , but you could well be correct

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/blob/main/docs/faq.md If you went straight from 10 to 30, it is probably set to 10 still. This makes it 22 after the PA the same as all the other boards. Power isn't everything, if you have line of sight it works with the smallest built in antenna which is emmiting less power in any given direction but the same total over all. (edited)
messages.content_html: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/blob/main/docs/faq.md If you went straight from 10 to 30, it is probably set to 10 still. 868MHz 7 Types High-Rejection Cavity Filters for EU Helium & LoRaWA...
DESCRIPTION ACASOM offers seven specialized types of 868MHz Cavity Filters to meet the exact demands of European Helium Network and LoRaWAN deployments. messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: Sorry missed this, the board has a small but significant difference so it just worn work yet. They have swapped the FEM from GC1109 to KCT8103L so the RF switch is a different pin new board is stuck in RX mode. But it can do more, like turning off the extra LNA. I found these https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1867 https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1857 And this flasher with the latest dev + the fix https://mcimages.weebl.me/?commitId=heltec_v4.3 If you try to transmit without it you probably wont hurt anything though. (edited)
messages.content_html: Sorry missed this, the board has a small but significant difference so it just worn work yet. They have swapped the FEM from GC1109 to KCT8103L so the RF switch is a different pin new board is stuck in RX mode. But it can do more, like turning off the extra LNA. I found these https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1867 https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1857 And this flasher with the latest dev + the fix https://mcimages.weebl.me/?commitId=heltec_v4.3 If you try to transmit without it you probably wont hurt anything though. (edited)

messages.text: nRF has limited space and the bluetooth messes up often. ESP was pretty reliable till the recent bug of orphaned files filling up the storage. Either way the fix when it happens is to erase and restore a backup. just in case there is file corruption.
